content.text: Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) for LLM vendors and AI integrators is the systematic process of identifying, assessing, and managing the risks introduced when an organization depends on external parties to deliver or process AI model capabilities. Unlike conventional software vendor risk, LLM vendor risk combines the operational dependencies of a critical SaaS supplier with distinctly AI-specific concerns: the vendor can silently update the underlying model weights, change the training data composition, alter safety guardrails, or rotate to a different underlying architecture — all without a conventional software release that would trigger standard change management controls.

attrs.alt: TPRM for LLM vendors: four-column layout showing vendor risk tiering, due diligence domains, contractual protections, and risk scoring output

content.text: What It Is

TPRM for LLM vendors is the application of third-party risk management controls to organizations that supply or integrate large language model capabilities into your AI stack. The scope covers: foundation model API providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Cohere, Mistral AI, AWS Bedrock), cloud-hosted fine-tuned or hosted model services (Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI), embedding and vector database providers (Pinecone, Weaviate, Cohere Embed), AI orchestration integrators that process your data (LangChain commercial, Dify, Flowise), and human feedback or annotation vendors who label AI training data.

A complete TPRM program for these vendors covers: initial risk tiering (determining how critical the vendor is to your operations), due diligence assessment (collecting and evaluating evidence about the vendor's security, data handling, and AI-specific practices), contractual protections (negotiating and executing the required legal instruments), and ongoing monitoring (continuous surveillance of the vendor's risk posture between formal reviews).
Why It Matters

Regulators have made explicit that AI vendors are within the scope of third-party risk management obligations. The OCC's Third-Party Relationships guidance (OCC 2023-17) states that banks must apply risk-based due diligence to third parties that use or provide AI models. The EU AI Act classifies providers of high-risk AI components as responsible parties in the supply chain. FRB SR 11-7 requires ongoing monitoring for models, whether developed internally or procured from third parties.

Beyond regulatory compliance, LLM vendors introduce operational risks that are materially different from conventional SaaS vendors. A conventional SaaS vendor outage interrupts a workflow; an LLM vendor outage can make an AI agent cease to function entirely and with no in-house substitute. More subtly, a vendor changing the underlying model can silently change the agent's behavior without any service interruption — an event that may not be visible until customer harm or a compliance failure surfaces.
Benefits
Regulatory compliance
— Satisfies OCC, FFIEC, and EU AI Act requirements for third-party due diligence on AI components and provides audit evidence for examination.
Operational resilience
— Identifies concentration risks and pushes the organization to develop fallback plans before a vendor outage creates an emergency.
Data protection assurance

— Contractual and due-diligence controls ensure that sensitive data submitted to LLM APIs is not used for model training by the vendor, is deleted within defined retention periods, and does not cross jurisdictional boundaries without authorization.
Change management visibility
— Model update notification requirements in the contract give advance warning of changes that could affect production agent behavior, enabling testing before cutover.
Negotiating leverage
— A structured TPRM questionnaire surfaces gaps in a vendor's controls before contract signature, when leverage to negotiate remediation commitments is highest.
Trade-offs
Vendor questionnaire limitations

— Large LLM vendors (OpenAI, Anthropic) typically do not complete bespoke questionnaires; they provide standardized documentation (CAIQ, SOC 2 report, trust portal documents). Assessors must adapt to working with vendor-supplied evidence rather than bespoke responses.
Contractual limitations with large vendors

— Foundation model providers have standardized terms of service that large enterprise customers can negotiate, but smaller customers typically cannot. Organizations with limited purchasing power may not be able to obtain ideal contractual protections.
AI-specific controls are nascent

— SOC 2 reports for AI vendors do not yet include model-specific trust service criteria. Assessors must evaluate model governance controls using supplementary questionnaires and press reports, not a standardized control mapping.
Concentration risk is structural
— The foundation model market has a small number of tier-1 providers. Achieving true multi-vendor redundancy may require accepting lower model quality or significantly higher complexity.
How It Works
The TPRM lifecycle for LLM vendors has five stages:
Risk tiering
— Classify each vendor into Tier 1 (Critical), Tier 2 (High), Tier 3 (Medium), or Tier 4 (Low) based on data sensitivity, business criticality, replaceability, and volume of transactions processed.
Due diligence

— Issue a vendor assessment questionnaire covering AI-specific domains (data handling, model training practices, security controls, SLAs, model change management, subprocessor management). Collect and review the vendor's SOC 2 Type II report, ISO 27001 certificate, and trust portal documentation.
SOC 2 report review

— Obtain and read the vendor's SOC 2 Type II report. Look for: (a) qualified opinions or exceptions in the auditor's report, (b) gaps in control coverage relative to the Trust Service Criteria, (c) complementary user entity controls (CUECs) that the customer must implement to complete the control, and (d) the review period and whether it covers the current engagement period.
Contractual execution

— Execute a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) or Data Processing Addendum that specifies: purpose limitation, data retention and deletion, subprocessor list and change notification, cross-border transfer mechanisms, breach notification SLA, model update notification commitments, and right to audit or receive audit reports.
Ongoing monitoring

— Monitor vendor incident disclosures, security bulletins, model update announcements, financial health indicators (for startup vendors), and changes to terms of service. Review Tier 1 vendors annually, Tier 2 biannually, Tier 3 every two years.
Architecture

A TPRM program for LLM vendors operates through three interlocking systems: the vendor inventory (tracking all AI vendors, their tier, assessment status, and review dates), the due diligence evidence repository (storing questionnaire responses, SOC 2 reports, certificates, and risk scoring outputs), and the contract register (tracking executed agreements, key terms, notification SLAs, and renewal dates). These systems feed a risk dashboard that surfaces vendors whose review is overdue, whose risk rating has changed, or whose contract terms are approaching renewal.
When to Use It
Before onboarding any new LLM vendor
— Due diligence must be completed before the vendor processes production data, not retroactively after deployment.
When a vendor introduces a material change
— Model version changes, new subprocessors, or changes to data handling practices should trigger a reassessment.
Annually for Tier 1 vendors
— Core LLM providers used for consequential decisions require annual full reassessment, including refreshed SOC 2 review and questionnaire update.
At contract renewal
— Renewal is the primary negotiation window. TPRM findings should drive contractual improvements before signature.
How to Implement

The following YAML represents a structured due diligence questionnaire template for an LLM vendor. Complete this template for each Tier 1 or Tier 2 vendor, supplemented with the vendor's own documentation:

# llm-vendor-assessment-questionnaire.yaml
# Complete for Tier 1 and Tier 2 LLM vendors before onboarding

vendor:
  name: "[VENDOR NAME]"
  products_in_scope: ["[API/Product 1]", "[API/Product 2]"]
  assessment_date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
  assessor: "[NAME, TITLE]"
  tier: null  # To be determined after scoring

# Section A: Data Handling
data_handling:
  training_data_opt_out:
    question: "Does the vendor train on customer-submitted prompts or completions by default?"
    acceptable_answer: "No, unless customer explicitly opts in."
    evidence: null
    finding: null

  data_retention:
    question: "What is the retention period for API inputs/outputs?"
    acceptable_answer: "<= 30 days for inputs, 0 for completions"
    evidence: null
    finding: null

  cross_border_transfers:
    question: "Are data processed in jurisdictions outside the customer's region?"
    acceptable_answer: "No, or SCCs/BCRs in place"
    evidence: null
    finding: null

  data_deletion:
    question: "Can customers request deletion of stored data, and what is the SLA?"
    acceptable_answer: "Yes, within 30 days"
    evidence: null
    finding: null

# Section B: Security Controls
security:
  soc2_type2:
    question: "Does the vendor hold a current SOC 2 Type II report covering Security, Availability, and Confidentiality?"
    evidence: null  # Path to obtained SOC 2 report
    opinion: null   # Unqualified / Qualified
    exceptions: null
    coverage_period_end: null

  iso_27001:
    question: "Is the vendor ISO 27001 certified? Scope and last audit date?"
    evidence: null
    finding: null

  penetration_testing:
    question: "How frequently are penetration tests conducted, and by whom?"
    acceptable_answer: "Annual minimum, by qualified third party"
    finding: null

  vulnerability_disclosure:
    question: "Does the vendor have a responsible disclosure or bug bounty program?"
    finding: null

# Section C: Availability and SLA
availability:
  api_uptime_sla:
    question: "What is the contractually committed API uptime SLA?"
    acceptable_answer: ">= 99.9% per month"
    finding: null

  incident_notification_sla:
    question: "What is the SLA for notifying customers of security incidents?"
    acceptable_answer: "<= 72 hours for confirmed breaches"
    finding: null

  dr_bcp:
    question: "Does the vendor have documented DR/BCP? Last tested?"
    acceptable_answer: "Yes, tested within 12 months"
    finding: null

# Section D: Model Change Management
model_change_management:
  update_notification:
    question: "How much advance notice does the vendor provide before model updates?"
    acceptable_answer: ">= 30 days advance notice for behavioral changes"
    finding: null

  version_pinning:
    question: "Can customers pin to a specific model version to avoid silent updates?"
    acceptable_answer: "Yes, for at least 6 months after deprecation notice"
    finding: null

  deprecation_timeline:
    question: "What is the minimum deprecation notice period for retired model versions?"
    acceptable_answer: ">= 6 months"
    finding: null

# Section E: Subprocessors
subprocessors:
  list_published:
    question: "Does the vendor publish a list of subprocessors?"
    evidence: null  # URL to subprocessor list
    finding: null

  change_notification:
    question: "How are customers notified of subprocessor changes?"
    acceptable_answer: ">= 30 days advance notice with objection right"
    finding: null
Examples

The following Python script implements a structured vendor risk scoring model that combines inherent risk factors, control effectiveness scores, and concentration risk to produce a residual risk rating:
The following JSON defines the contractual requirements checklist used to verify a vendor's Data Processing Agreement before signature:

{
  "contract_checklist": {
    "vendor": "[VENDOR NAME]",
    "agreement_type": "Data Processing Agreement / Addendum",
    "review_date": "YYYY-MM-DD",
    "requirements": [
      {
        "id": "DPA-01",
        "category": "Purpose Limitation",
        "requirement": "Agreement explicitly prohibits vendor from using customer data for model training or improvement without opt-in consent.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": "Negotiate explicit prohibition clause before signature."
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-02",
        "category": "Data Retention",
        "requirement": "Agreement specifies a maximum retention period for API inputs of 30 days and requires deletion confirmation on request within 30 days.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": "Add specific retention and deletion SLA exhibit."
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-03",
        "category": "Breach Notification",
        "requirement": "Vendor must notify customer of a confirmed security breach within 72 hours of the vendor becoming aware.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": "Insert specific notification SLA; vendor's standard terms typically state 'promptly' without a defined SLA."
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-04",
        "category": "Model Update Notification",
        "requirement": "Vendor must provide at least 30 days advance notice of model version changes that may materially alter output behavior.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": "Add model change management addendum specific to LLM services."
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-05",
        "category": "Right to Audit",
        "requirement": "Customer has the right to audit, or to receive annual third-party audit reports (SOC 2 Type II), within 30 days of request.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": null
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-06",
        "category": "Subprocessors",
        "requirement": "Vendor publishes subprocessor list and provides 30 days advance notice of additions with customer objection right.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": null
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-07",
        "category": "Cross-Border Transfers",
        "requirement": "Agreement identifies data transfer mechanisms for international transfers (SCCs, adequacy decision, BCRs).",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": null
      },
      {
        "id": "DPA-08",
        "category": "Exit and Portability",
        "requirement": "On termination, vendor returns all customer data in machine-readable format within 30 days and provides 90 days of transition assistance.",
        "present": null,
        "clause_reference": null,
        "gap_action": null
      }
    ]
  }
}
Real-World Example

A regulated financial institution onboarded an LLM API provider for a customer-facing investment education agent. The TPRM team's initial assessment found three gaps: (1) the vendor's standard DPA did not include a model update notification requirement, (2) the SOC 2 report covered only Security and Availability (not Confidentiality), and (3) the vendor's stated data retention was "up to 30 days" with no deletion-on-request SLA.

The team used the assessment findings as leverage in contract negotiations. They obtained: a model change addendum requiring 30-day advance notice of behavioral model updates; an updated DPA with explicit training data opt-out and data deletion within 14 days of request; and a commitment to expand the next SOC 2 report to include Confidentiality. The vendor also agreed to provide a quarterly summary of security incidents affecting the API. This example illustrates that TPRM findings, when documented in a formal assessment, become the negotiation basis for contractual improvements that would otherwise be unavailable.
Common Mistakes
Treating LLM vendors as standard SaaS
— Standard SaaS TPRM questionnaires do not cover model training data practices, model version management, or AI-specific safety controls. A specialized LLM vendor addendum is required.
Accepting SOC 2 without reviewing scope and exceptions
— A SOC 2 badge on a vendor's website means only that a report exists. Assessors must read the opinion, check scope, and identify CUECs the organization must implement.
Ignoring concentration risk
— Organizations that route 100% of LLM workloads through one provider with no fallback plan have unacceptable concentration risk, regardless of how good that provider's controls are.
Failing to include model change notification in the contract
— This is the single most common and impactful gap. Without contractual notification requirements, the vendor can silently update model versions and the customer has no recourse.
No ongoing monitoring cadence

— Initial due diligence without a defined re-assessment schedule creates a point-in-time snapshot that becomes stale. Vendor risk profiles change; a vendor that was low-risk at onboarding may have materially changed two years later.
Best Practices
Build an LLM vendor addendum to your standard TPRM questionnaire

— Add sections covering model training data practices, version management, AI safety controls, and model-specific SLAs. This can be issued as a supplementary questionnaire even when the vendor does not complete a full DDQ.
Require SOC 2 Type II with annual refresh
— Accept only Type II (not Type I); require that the audit period end date is within the last 12 months; verify the SOC 2 covers the specific service used (not a broader scope that excludes the API).
Develop a multi-vendor fallback plan for Tier 1 providers

— Maintain an abstraction layer (e.g., LiteLLM, a proxy router, or the agent's own provider-agnostic interface) that allows routing to a secondary provider in the event of outage or termination. Test this fallback annually.
Negotiate model change notifications contractually, not just informally
— A vendor's blog post about model updates is not a contractual notification. The DPA must specify the notification mechanism, minimum notice period, and what constitutes a material model change.
Track CUECs explicitly

— When the SOC 2 report lists CUECs, assign each CUEC to a named control owner in your organization and evidence that the CUEC is implemented. Unimplemented CUECs represent control gaps even though the vendor's SOC 2 is clean.
Monitor vendor financial health for startup AI vendors

— AI vendor startups have elevated failure risk. Monitor funding rounds, news, and financial disclosures. Have a contingency plan for vendor failure, including data retrieval and rapid re-procurement.
Performance & Scalability

A TPRM program for a mid-sized AI stack with 5–15 LLM vendors is manageable with a dedicated vendor risk analyst supported by a GRC platform (ServiceNow, Archer, LogicGate, or Vanta). For programs with more than 20 AI vendors, automate the evidence collection workflow: use vendor portals (Coupa, Whistic, OneTrust Vendor Risk) to request and receive questionnaire responses and SOC 2 reports digitally, with automated scoring against the defined criteria. This reduces assessment time from weeks to days per vendor at scale.
Tooling & Ecosystem
GRC platforms
— ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate, Vanta, Drata, Whistic. Manage the vendor inventory, assessment workflows, evidence storage, and risk ratings at scale.
Vendor trust portals
— OpenAI Trust Portal, Anthropic Trust Center, Azure Trust Center, AWS Artifact. Primary sources for SOC 2 reports, ISO certificates, and DPA templates for major LLM providers.
Contract lifecycle management (CLM)
— Ironclad, Icertis, DocuSign CLM. Track executed agreements, key terms, and renewal dates. Configure alerts for upcoming contract renewals and notification SLA breaches.
CAIQ / SIG questionnaires

— The Cloud Security Alliance's CAIQ (Consensus Assessments Initiative Questionnaire) and Shared Assessments SIG are standardized questionnaire frameworks that many vendors will complete. Use as the base for AI vendor questionnaires, augmented with AI-specific sections.
Comparison with Alternatives

Self-hosted or on-premises LLM deployment eliminates vendor dependency and provides full control over model updates, but introduces infrastructure and MLOps burden, requires access to hardware (GPU clusters), and may not be cost-effective for smaller organizations. A private cloud deployment of open-source models (Llama, Mistral) via providers like Replicate or Modal reduces third-party risk but still introduces infrastructure vendor risk. Full reliance on a single foundation model API provider minimizes complexity but maximizes concentration risk. A hybrid approach — primary provider plus a secondary provider maintained in shadow mode — balances cost and resilience.
Security & Reliability
Prompt injection via vendor-processed data
— Data submitted to vendor APIs may be logged by the vendor. Ensure that sensitive data submitted as context is within scope of the vendor's confidentiality controls (confirmed via SOC 2 and DPA).
API key security

— LLM API keys grant direct access to the vendor's services and billing. Store in secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, Azure Key Vault), rotate quarterly, and audit usage logs for anomalous consumption.
Vendor incident disclosure monitoring

— Subscribe to vendor security bulletins and status pages. Major providers (OpenAI, Anthropic) publish status at status.openai.com / status.anthropic.com. Alert on any incident that affects the services in scope for your deployment.
Mental Model

Think of an LLM vendor as a silent model developer embedded in your AI stack. Unlike traditional software vendors who hand over a compiled binary that does not change, an LLM provider hands you an API key that connects to a living model that they update on their own schedule. TPRM for LLM vendors is the process of treating that silent model developer as the consequential third party they are: verifying their controls, negotiating their obligations in writing, and monitoring their performance continuously. The unique wrinkle is that the "product" the vendor delivers can change its behavior without any deployment action on your part — making the model change notification clause in the contract among the most important protections in the vendor relationship.
Key Takeaways

Tier LLM vendors by criticality before selecting the depth of due diligence: core model providers are Tier 1 and require full assessment including SOC 2 review, DPA negotiation, and annual reassessment.
The five due diligence domains for LLM vendors are: data handling, security controls, availability and SLA, model change management, and subprocessors.
Contractual model change notification (minimum 30 days advance notice) is the single most important LLM-specific protection to negotiate; standard vendor terms typically do not include it.
SOC 2 Type II reports must be read, not just obtained. Verify the opinion, scope, exceptions, CUEC list, and that the coverage period is current.
Concentration risk is structural in the LLM market; develop a multi-vendor fallback plan for any vendor handling more than 50% of production LLM workloads.
TPRM findings documented in formal assessments become negotiation leverage at contract signature and renewal — this is the highest-ROI stage for driving vendor control improvements.
